item.title: Crystal LSP implementation for Windows and Linux
item.author: /u/No_1944
item.content:encoded: Since Crystalline only supports some POSIX platforms, I wrote an LSP implementation which works cross platform.

item.title: Crystal spec is VERY slow and very high CPU usage when the first time run `crystal spec -O1`
item.author: zw963
item.content:encoded: Please check following screenshot.Why the first time run spec with -O1 is so slow?(15 min on a very little shard) I almost think about crystal spec is in the dead loop.

item.title: Vibe-coding in Crystal
item.author: renich
item.content:encoded: I know vibe-coding is, at the same time, a popular and unpopular topic among programmers. Many see it as some new kind of zero-code programming, others as a powerful “autocompletion on steroids” tool.In any case, I’d like to know what people here think about it. If you have used it and which problems have you faced.I’ve done it a lot, honestly. I have many projects in Crystal that I’ve tried to develop using opencode.ai and gemini-cli.My first paint point is that Crystal is not a mainstream language yet. That makes it complicated. Especially to use idiomatic crystal with agents.That said, I have some projects that have “taken off” meaning that they actually work, even if I use them myself only. Others have been just impossible to code.So, what’s up? Have you tried doing this? Is vibe-coding the devil in disguise? What have you stumbled upon?

item.title: Announcing CrymbleUI - a modern, native Crystal GUI framework
item.author: wolfgang371
item.content:encoded: ever missed a Crystal native GUI framework? Maybe you want to check out CrymbleUI.A nice and fast GUI framework for Crystal. Declarative and reactive. Its name is a pun of Crystal and nimble CrymbleUI’s first line of code emerged 2.11.2025, 19:46. Currently with SFML backend and for Linux. But easily portable to e.g. Windows.

item.title: RFC: Standard Image Types in Stdlib
item.author: naqvis
item.content:encoded:
Add standard image types (colors, pixel formats, color spaces) to Crystal’s standard library to provide a common foundation for the image processing ecosystem.This RFC is meant to start a discussion about standardizing image types in Crystal. If the community finds value in this approach, anyone interested can help move it forward.The Problem: Ecosystem FragmentationCrystal’s image ecosystem is fragmented. Each library defines its own incompatible types:struct RGBA property r, g, b, a : UInt16 end module Color struct RGBA property r, g, b, a : UInt8 end end struct Color property r, g, b, a : UInt8 end Developers must learn different APIs for each libraryNo shared understanding of color representationUnclear semantics (premultiplied? color space? bit depth?)Difficult to build tools that work across librariesEach library reinvents basic conceptsComparison to Other Languages - Standard library provides foundation:// In stdlib package image/color
type RGBA struct { R, G, B, A uint8 }
type RGBA64 struct { R, G, B, A uint16 } All Go image libraries use these types. - Community standard via crate:pub trait Pixel { type Subpixel: Primitive; const CHANNEL_COUNT: u8; }
pub struct Rgba<T>([T; 4]); pub struct Rgb<T>([T; 3]); Most Rust image libraries implement these traits. - PIL/Pillow provides foundation:from PIL import Image
# Standard modes: "RGB", "RGBA", "L" (grayscale), etc. img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height)) pixel = img.getpixel((x, y)) # Returns (r, g, b) tuple Most Python image libraries interoperate via PIL’s standard modes. - Java stdlib provides foundation:// In java.awt BufferedImage img = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_INT_ARGB); int rgb = img.getRGB(x, y); // Standard packed ARGB format Kotlin and Android use or .A that defines:Standard color types with clear semanticsStandard pixel format definitionsStandard buffer interfaceStandard color space definitionsThis allows libraries to:Share a common vocabularyBuild on a solid foundationWhen all libraries use the standard foundation, you have consistency:require "image" # Standard foundation
# All libraries use the same color types lib_a_color = Image::RGBA8.new(255, 0, 0, 255) lib_b_color = Image::RGBA8.new(255, 0, 0, 255)
# Same type everywhere lib_a_color == lib_b_color # true Build on the foundation instead of reinventing:require "image"
module MyImageLib class Canvas @pixels : Slice(Image::RGBA8)
def [](x, y) : Image::RGBA8 @pixels[y * @width + x] end end end Proposed Standard Library AdditionAdd module to Crystal stdlib (or create minimal shard):module Image # 8-bit RGBA (premultiplied alpha) struct RGBA8 getter r : UInt8 getter g : UInt8 getter b : UInt8 getter a : UInt8
def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 255_u8) end
# Premultiplied alpha semantics # Valid range: 0 <= r,g,b <= a <= 255 end
# 8-bit RGBA (non-premultiplied alpha) struct NRGBA8 getter r : UInt8 getter g : UInt8 getter b : UInt8 getter a : UInt8
def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 255_u8) end end
# 16-bit RGBA (premultiplied alpha) struct RGBA16 getter r : UInt16 getter g : UInt16 getter b : UInt16 getter a : UInt16
def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 65535_u16) end end
# 16-bit RGBA (non-premultiplied alpha) struct NRGBA16 getter r : UInt16 getter g : UInt16 getter b : UInt16 getter a : UInt16
def initialize(@r, @g, @b, @a = 65535_u16) end end
# 8-bit RGB (no alpha) struct RGB8 getter r : UInt8 getter g : UInt8 getter b : UInt8
def initialize(@r, @g, @b) end end
# 8-bit grayscale struct Gray8 getter y : UInt8
def initialize(@y) end end
# 16-bit grayscale struct Gray16 getter y : UInt16
def initialize(@y) end end end module Image # Standard color spaces enum ColorSpace # sRGB (IEC 61966-2-1) - default for most images SRGB
# Linear RGB - for compositing and blending LinearRGB
# Adobe RGB (1998) - wider gamut AdobeRGB
# Display P3 - modern wide gamut displays DisplayP3 end end module Image # Describes pixel encoding in memory enum PixelFormat RGBA8 # 4 bytes: R, G, B, A (premultiplied) NRGBA8 # 4 bytes: R, G, B, A (non-premultiplied) RGB8 # 3 bytes: R, G, B GRAY8 # 1 byte: Y
RGBA16 # 8 bytes: R, G, B, A (premultiplied, little-endian) NRGBA16 # 8 bytes: R, G, B, A (non-premultiplied, little-endian) RGB16 # 6 bytes: R, G, B (little-endian) GRAY16 # 2 bytes: Y (little-endian)
def bytes_per_pixel : Int32 case self in .rgba8?, .nrgba8? then 4 in .rgb8? then 3 in .gray8? then 1 in .rgba16?, .nrgba16? then 8 in .rgb16? then 6 in .gray16? then 2 end end
def premultiplied? : Bool case self in .rgba8?, .rgba16? then true else false end end end end module Image # Axis-aligned rectangle struct Rectangle getter min : Point getter max : Point
def initialize(@min, @max) end
def width : Int32 max.x - min.x end
def height : Int32 max.y - min.y end end
struct Point getter x : Int32 getter y : Int32
def initialize(@x, @y) end end end Buffer Interface (Minimal)module Image # Minimal interface for pixel buffers # Libraries can implement this or provide their own richer APIs module Buffer abstract def width : Int32 abstract def height : Int32 abstract def pixel_format : PixelFormat abstract def color_space : ColorSpace
# Optional: direct buffer access # abstract def to_unsafe : Pointer(UInt8) # abstract def stride : Int32 end end : Only essential types, not operations: Explicit about premultiplication, bit depth, endianness: Different types for different formats: Libraries can add their own typesWhat This Does NOT IncludeImage I/O (PNG, JPEG, etc.) - libraries handle thisImage operations (resize, filter, etc.) - libraries handle thisFormat conversion - libraries handle thisDrawing primitives - libraries handle thisThis is - the building blocks.Libraries can adopt incrementally:: Use standard color types internally# In any library alias RGBA = Image::RGBA16 # Use standard type : Expose standard types in APIdef [](x, y) : Image::RGBA16 : Implement standard interfacesclass Canvas include Image::Buffer end : Adds types to standard library: Existing libraries must migrate: Doesn’t cover all pixel formats: Makes choices about representationWhy in stdlib (or minimal shard)?Always available, no dependenciesStable API, semantic versioningCommunity standard by defaultLike Go’s packageMinimal shard alternative:Can be adopted before stdlib inclusionWhy these specific types?: Most common formatsPremultiplied vs non-premultiplied: Both needed, different use cases: Prevents confusionAlternative 1: Do nothingLet ecosystem remain fragmented.: Problem will worsen as more libraries emergeAlternative 2: Pick one library as standardMake everyone use one specific library’s types.: Creates monopoly, not neutralAlternative 3: Comprehensive image libraryBuild full-featured stdlib image library.: Too large for stdlib, limits innovationAlternative 4: Just protocols/interfacesDefine interfaces without concrete types.: Doesn’t solve the “every library has different RGBA” problempackage color
type Color interface { RGBA() (r, g, b, a uint32) }
type RGBA struct { R, G, B, A uint8 } : All Go image libraries use these typespub trait Pixel { type Subpixel; const CHANNEL_COUNT: u8; }
pub struct Rgba<T>([T; 4]); : De facto standard in Rust ecosystemfrom PIL import Image
# Standard image modes img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height)) img = Image.new("RGBA", (width, height)) img = Image.new("L", (width, height)) # Grayscale : Standard modes used across Python image ecosystempublic class Color { public Color(int r, int g, int b, int a) } : Standard in Java ecosystemStdlib or separate shard?Stdlib: More authoritative, always availableShard: Faster iteration, easier adoptionCurrent proposal: RGBA8, NRGBA8, RGBA16, NRGBA16, RGB8, Gray8, Gray16Missing: CMYK, YCbCr, HSV, LAB - add later?Enum sufficient or need more metadata?Assume little-endian or make explicit?Include in foundation or leave to libraries?How to encourage library adoption?Migration guide for existing librariesDocumentation and examplesImage operations (resize, filter, etc.)Format I/O (PNG, JPEG readers/writers)Color space conversion algorithmsstruct CMYK8 struct YCbCr8 struct HSV struct LAB module Image def self.convert(color : RGBA8, to : ColorSpace) : RGBA8 end module Image class SimpleBuffer include Buffer # Reference implementation end end module Image def self.register_format(name : String, reader : FormatReader) end But these are - start with minimal foundation.10 posts - 4 participants

item.title: Jelly VM - A BEAM clone, maybe better
item.author: /u/Fabulous-Repair-8665
item.content:encoded: Wrote it back in the May when I wanted to dive deeper into Erlang, continued it recently and turned out great, all it needs now is a programming language.It is stack based, supports fault tolerance primitives and lightweight processes, a lot more flexible and comfortable to debug than BEAM. It is not "battle tested" at all, that is up to you :P
